sentences.text: I was born in Lodz in the year 1922.
On the 8th of August.
On the 8th of August.
Who was in your family?
We were a family of five, my parents and my two sisters.
And we lived in a one-room apartment, the five of us.
My parents were working people.
Naturally, we were a working-class family.
Life was hard, but we were a happy family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was a political-orientated man, and so was my mother.
And although things were very difficult materially, I cannot remember my parents not singing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We also were readers.
All of our family read constantly.
We belonged to libraries.
But we also had a small bookshelf.
And I remember when father, whenever he bought a book, it was like a-- a celebration at home.
Mother put an extra piece of meat on the plate.
When we had a book, everybody had the book.
It was something like an extra member came into the family.
My father, Gershon, Gershon Rosenberg.
And my mother, Masha.
I had a sister, Pola, and then, Ida, the younger one.
I was actually the youngest member of the family.
My sister, the younger one, was four years older than me.
And the older one, eight years older than me.
So actually when the-- when the war broke out, my younger sister was 21.
And my older sister was 25.
She was already married, and had a little baby, [INAUDIBLE] was her name.
He was a textile-- a weaver in a textile factory, and so was my mother.
They both worked in one factory for about 30 years.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they eked out a living from-- from being factory people, factory hand.
My sister, my both sisters already worked at this stage, and I was still a school boy, or just finished school.
I was 17 at the outbreak of war, so maybe about two years after-- after school.
I went to a Yiddish school, under the name of Vladimir Madame.
It was an excellent school.
Perhaps academically we were almost like a high school.
My teachers were my friends.
There is not-- there is not another school-- or I-- I haven't heard of another school, which you can equate with that, with my school.
My teacher taught me in the morning geography, history, arithmetic.
And in the evening, he was my librarian.
So in fact, he had a total control of my intellectual development.
He knew exactly what I was reading, because reading was my free choice.
Education wasn't free.
Education was a part of the curriculum.
But I-- when I went to the library, this was my domain.
So, in fact, it was sort of a-- a whole academia took place for the few years as I attended this school.
And furthermore, my teachers were also my instructors on camp.
I used to go on camp, on summer camp.
And they were there too.
So you can imagine they were part of-- like a family.
What did you like to read most of all?
History and literature.
I was always interested in history and literature, literature more than anything else, poetry.
And my major reading was most probably Peretz, Sholem Aleichem, and-- and Singer, Zalman Shneur, books like this.
I also read a lot of Yiddish translation.
People would not-- cannot understand how a boy from a Yiddish school was acquainted with Shakespeare.
They couldn't understand that Shakespeare was a-- a matter of fact when I came to Australia, and somebody wanted to sort of intimidate me, on the account of Yiddish speaking.
And I said to him, look, I was very pleasantly surprised when I came to Australia, and I found that Shakespeare is translated in English, too.
He looked at me.
He gave me a funny look.
I said, yes, yes.
I read Shakespeare in Yiddish.
He would not-- could not-- but we had it.
We had Shakespeare, and [INAUDIBLE], and Kafka, and-- and-- and Flaubert.
And you name it, and Balzac, we had it all in Yiddish.
And most probably the children of my vintage, young boys and girls, were by 16 or 17 years a-- completely acquainted with the Russian and French classics.
We had Pushkin, and Chekhov, and Turgenev, and-- and Tolstoy, and Dostoyevsky, and Flaubert and Balzac, and-- and Victor Hugo.
We read them all.
We knew them all.
We spoke Yiddish at home.
Yiddish was the major language.
But my sisters also spoke Polish.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After all, we-- Polish was part of-- of-- you know, it was the language of the country.
And Polish was spoken, but not to the extent that we-- one could say that we linguistic-- were we linguistically assimilated?
No.
It was in a factory owned by Jews.
But it was-- there were Germans, and Poles, and Ukrainians.
And a matter of fact, my father was their delegate, their delegate.
He was delegated from the workers to negotiate with the-- with the boss about the conditions and so on.
But it was not an exclusive Yiddish factory just-- just for Jews.
My father was a-- a-- a Bundist.
He was active in the professional and in the textile movement.
He was president of the textile union, which was quite a prestigious position, the first legal-- legal textile union in Poland, he was president.
He was also elected chair-- chairman of the court of-- of a-- a friendship court, they called it.
It was camaraderie.
He was elected.
And they-- of course, a lot of our people, especially the politically-orientated people, did not go to court to settle their-- their problems.
They came to their own friends, and my father was the president of that-- of that court.
For 20 years, he was-- he was reelected every year, year after year.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
He undertook negotiations.
He also pronounced judgment, which was obeyed.
And the-- the problems were family problems, between husband and wife, between children and parents, and so on.
And somehow people didn't feel to go to court.
It was expensive.
And there was a mistrust to the judiciary, quite justified.
It was under the auspices of the textile union.
This is a good question.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- we were not religious people.
There was a certain tradition kept alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I-- I don't believe that language on its own is determining one's nationality.
Although language is a very important part, I feel that language is in a sense the spiritual border of the physical existence of a people.
Without that border, without language, no language, no people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
But I think that Jewishness was not a question of-- of an academic explanation.
It was just we were.
You know, if some-- if some person would have come to my street and asked a little boy, tell me are you Jewish or who is a Jew here?
He would go to his father and say, there's a [YIDDISH], a crazy man outside asking me who was a Jew.
It would never occur to him that no one else was-- that there is not a Jew.
It was just a natural phenomenon.
So did you live in an area that was all Jewish?
Yes, yes, predominantly Jewish, yeah.
It was not so good.
It was not so good, especially when I was a-- a youngster, the relations were not good.
I believe that had a lot to do with the-- with the Nazi system or Germany, Germany's influence on Poland.
Perhaps the Polish authorities and the Polish people did not realize that the antisemitism imported from Germany.
Although they didn't-- they don't need to be imported.
They had-- they were antisemites as well.

But they understand that their particular German antisemitism which was imported from-- in back from the '30s, in '30 from perhaps 1930 to 1939, was actually the harbinger of the-- the German invasion.
They-- they morally destroyed the Polish people.
And they-- they made them look that their most important enemy is not Germany, who prepares war against them, but a few Jews who live amongst them.
And this augmented, I think, the antisemitism that they had already.
It gave them-- it gave antisemitism a political tool.
Before it was only economical, and perhaps religious.
But on those particular years, I feel that it was Germany, it was Nazi Germany, who forged this political antisemitic tool in Europe.
Yes, I did.
I did.
I did.
We lived, let's say, in a certain segment of my street.
I could only go to a certain number.
And I remember the number was 30.
After-- after 30, if I-- we lived number 10.
If I would go past number 30, I took my life into my own hands.
Yeah, yeah.
She was more traditional.
But, you know, she was influenced.
I think my father was a stronger personality.
And my father-- and my father and my mother, I-- I-- sometimes they talk about here generation gap.
My grandparents, my both-- my both zaides, my both grandfathers, who were deep, traditional religious people, they could not understand.
And all of a sudden they have a son and a daughter, who don't want to have a [NON-ENGLISH], who became just the opposite, who had rebelled at everything that-- which was traditional.
And-- and-- and in their rebellion, the animosity was so strong that they also lost the charm and the beauty of-- of traditionally Jewish life, which was trampled over by them.
They-- they-- they could not find a-- a-- a gold middle.
Do you understand?
Did you celebrate any Jewish festivals?
We celebrated Pesach, but also we gave it an modern explanation.
My father was a yeshiva broker before he was-- became a political-orientated man.
And he-- he knew how to-- how to explain.

And-- and in fact, I believe now, although I am not anymore political-- I have nothing to do with political movements-- I believe that Judaism, the way I understand it, was primarily a secular philosophy.
Religiosity became-- was part-- came later.
That's the way I understand.
If you-- if you divorce from Judaism, the mythology, you still retain a viable philosophy by which men can build a-- a new life, a-- a just society.
That's the way I understand it.

My father was a universalist, and he believed in brotherhood, and that tomorrow-- after tomorrow, this modern Messiah will come, and-- and we'll be having new life, and brotherhood which will-- utopian.
It-- I-- I believe-- it's my belief that most of Jewish Bundists, their whole political attitude was rather it came-- came a-- a-- a new understanding of humanism.

I think it was-- it was in a sense an Erasmian attitude to a new society, not the-- the Bolshevik, and not the Communist, not even the sort of a-- I-- I really can't-- this is-- this is something which most probably was part of the-- the horizon, an Erasmian society, a humanistic society, where people will be able to live in peace with one another.
And there will be this eternal brotherhood, which was also prophesized by the prophets, by Jeremiahs and Isaiahs, and-- and all of-- Amoses, all those.
This was there.
I-- I think that it actually was the old philosophy in a new form.
The same thing I believe.
I never told [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the people I came in contact anything about comrade.
And this in itself is not just a-- a name, something you say.
It was a concept, comrade.
Like we talked about, when I was a boy, we talked about the French Social Democratic Party, about Leon Blum, who knew about me as much as I knew-- as I knew about the moon.
But I called him chaver Leon Blum, comrade Leon Blum.
I remember during the Austrian uprising in Schutzbund, uprised against the-- the fascist system in Austria.
There were people like leaders, Koloman Wallisch, Julius Deutsch, [?
Munichreiter, ?] [? and Weissel. ?] I always called them comrade.
And-- and this was something which, as I mentioned before, not a name.
It was an attitude, this warmth I felt over those people.
Yes, my sister, the older one, was even more extreme.
She was a communist.
And she spent many, many years in prisons for fighting and worshipping Stalin.
And what he did to her, well, she was-- she-- she lost her life because of the belief in-- in the Communist system.
She really did in the end.
Not so good, not so good.
My father was suffering terribly from the fact that she's a Communist, not because she had different beliefs, but to the danger she was exposed.
After all, a girls about 18, 19 she was constantly imprisoned.
And the Polish prisons were not paradise.
No prison is paradise.
But Polish prisons were one of the worst in-- in Eastern Europe, notoriously worse.
So that's what it is.
Yes.
I couldn't visit her.
But I-- I used to bring food.
My mother used to cook, and-- I remember we were not very wealthy people.
And she was with six other girls in one cell.
And we had to cook for the seven of them.
Because she wouldn't eat alone.
My younger sister was more pleasant.
I described her in one of my poems as a dove.
I described in my poem, I said, when I talk about religion, I had two sisters.
One was the dove, and one was the rebel.
I get to this sort of later on, perhaps.
I had all sorts of dreams.
But when do you-- what-- what age do you turn?
16, mother was still involved in very much in-- in-- in the Bundist movement.
And this was my dream.
That's a good question.
You know, it's-- it's nearly 50 years, 60 years.
I'm not anymore the same man, you know.
And I-- I-- it's difficult for me to go back and-- and respond to it question like this.
It looked-- I thought it's one of the biggest cities in the world, you know, because I've never been anywhere else.
It was a dirty city full of smoke, a poor city, hardworking people.
But there was life.
There was life.
There were beautiful gardens.
Of course, Jews had no access to them.
They wouldn't let us in.
And it-- it really sort of-- it didn't have any specific character, like any other cities, like Warsaw, or Krakow.
And it was-- it seems to me that in the beginning of-- of-- when Lodz was built, some-- a few people came and throw a heap of bricks into the sky, and wherever they fell down, there was a house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was nothing-- nothing special.
Very vibrant, very vibrant.
We-- we had-- we had libraries, and theater, and orchestra, and music.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- the desire to know, to understand, to be part of the-- of the universal knowledge of this big cosmic culture amongst young people is beyond comprehension, beyond.
They had no schools.
They could not go to schools.
Universities were prohibited.
High schools were only for the rich.
But I could never-- I-- as far as I can remember, girls, instead of bags of this-- bags they were here.
What do they call it?
I forgot the name of those-- those, they-- they under their arm was a book.
And boys had books.
And when they got together, the first question is, what are you reading now?
And how do you respond to-- to so-and-so?
And what is about the poetry?
And have you seen the latest theater play?
Now, this was the dialogue.
They didn't ask, where did you go on holiday?
Or-- or what car you're buying?
This was sort of-- you know, this-- this was not a-- it had no-- not-- it wasn't on the agenda.
But culture was something which was almost like they were paranoiac about-- about culture.
What aspects of the cultural life were you involved with?
I was involved in-- in reading, and in-- I was already sort of starting to write.
When I was a boy, I started-- when-- at school level.
I remember my first work.
I wrote a short story about the Polish poet writer.
His name was Jan Kochanowski, a famous-- very famous, very sensitive, very romantic writer.
And I wrote my first essay.
And it was read in front of the school.
And it was-- I was very-- as a young boy, I was-- I was elated.
Not at all, not at all, no.
I didn't know that this was going to affect my life.
This was-- it seemed to me so distant.
Some people-- some-- some of my friends understood.
But I couldn't comprehend it.
I thought that somewhere-- on another planet it's taking place.
That it will-- this will never happen in Poland.
I myself, despite my being so entrenched in Jewish life, and a certain-- maybe parochialism, maybe, physically but not spiritually.
I felt myself a Pole, a Polish patriot.
I was ready to fight for-- for Poland.
I felt it's my country.
It's my culture.
It's my history, my literature.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, it wasn't to be.
This enchantment was tragic.
As soon as the war broke out.
There was a tremendous division between us and the Poles.
[SIGHS] They-- they-- I can't understand.
It's something which needs someone perhaps like a man like Freud to analyze collectively what took place.
After all, Germany was a common enemy.
How could they side with them against us?
Of course, there were individuals.
But as a nation, as a collective, their-- their response to their Jewish brothers was miserable, morally degrading.
I think they-- they did not stood the test of time.
